_meta.description: Live grounded catalog — seed capabilities + pipeline-verified products.

capabilities.name: Flagship model
capabilities.description: Each provider's most capable general-purpose model.

capabilities.name: Consumer chat assistant
capabilities.description: Primary conversational app for end users across web, desktop, and mobile.

capabilities.name: Agentic coding
capabilities.description: Autonomous or assisted software development across terminal, IDE, and cloud surfaces.

capabilities.name: Remote / async agent control
capabilities.description: Starting, monitoring, and directing an agent (esp. a coding agent) from a remote device or asynchronously.
capabilities.comparison_note: Anthropic surfaces async control through interface-native handoffs (Claude Code on the web, Move tasks between web and terminal, Claude Code in Slack), OpenAI through infrastructure-native execution (Remote connections, Cloud environments, Codex Security cloud), and Google through orchestration-native delegation (Asynchronous Subagents, Multi-Agent Teamwork).

capabilities.name: Browser / computer-use agent
capabilities.description: An agent that drives a browser or computer on the user's behalf to complete multi-step web tasks.

capabilities.name: Knowledge-work / research agent
capabilities.description: Non-developer agent for documents, analysis, and research grounded in user sources.

capabilities.name: Agent-building SDK / framework
capabilities.description: Developer toolkit for building, orchestrating, and deploying custom agents.

capabilities.name: Enterprise agent platform / deployment
capabilities.description: Managed platform for building, deploying, governing, and running agents and models at enterprise scale.

capabilities.name: Image / video generation
capabilities.description: First-party models or products that generate images or video.

capabilities.name: Managed agent runtime
capabilities.description: Provider-hosted execution of agents: the provider runs the agent loop and sandboxes its tools, instead of you self-hosting.
capabilities.comparison_note: For managed agent runtime, Anthropic is sandbox-native, isolating execution via Sandbox runtime alongside Routines and Claude Code on the web; OpenAI is policy-native, governing runtime through Automations and its Permissions and security model; Google is project-native, structuring runtime around Projects, Sidecars, Subagents, and Hooks.

capabilities.name: MCP & connectors
capabilities.description: Model Context Protocol support: hosted MCP servers, connector registries, and managed tool connectors.
capabilities.comparison_note: Anthropic centers MCP on managed configuration (managed-mcp.json) and installation scopes, OpenAI treats MCP as transport-and-server-native (server transports, Codex as an MCP server), while Google leans store-native distribution via an MCP Store and mcp permission.

capabilities.name: Agent memory
capabilities.description: Persistent, cross-session memory an agent can read and write across runs.
capabilities.comparison_note: For agent memory, Anthropic is file-native (CLAUDE.md, auto-managed MEMORY.md, Output styles), OpenAI relies on a single file-native AGENTS.md, while Google takes a config-native approach through structured Rules and Workflows.

capabilities.name: Code execution & sandbox
capabilities.description: Server-side code execution in an isolated sandbox for agent actions.
capabilities.comparison_note: Anthropic offers container-native isolation through Dev containers and Docker-backed Containers, OpenAI takes a policy-native approach with Sandbox modes, Permission profiles, and Approval policy, while Google centers on terminal-native control via Terminal Sandboxing and a Fine-Grained Permissions Engine.

capabilities.name: Agent evals & observability
capabilities.description: Tracing, evaluation, and monitoring of agent runs.
capabilities.comparison_note: For agent evals and observability, Anthropic is telemetry-native via OpenTelemetry monitoring, cost tracking, and debug logging; OpenAI is platform-native with Tracing, Trace grading, an Evals API, and Datasets and eval runs; Google is runtime-native through Observability, Subagents, and Lifecycle Hooks.

capabilities.name: Guardrails & safety
capabilities.description: Policy and safety controls on an agent's inputs, outputs, and actions.
capabilities.comparison_note: Anthropic centers safety on a config-driven Permission system with Permission modes and Managed settings, OpenAI on runtime isolation via Sandbox mode plus an Approval policy and Permission profiles, while Google takes a policy-native approach through its Fine-Grained Permissions Engine, Allowlist/Denylist, and Strict Mode.

capabilities.name: Subagents & orchestration
capabilities.description: Spawning and coordinating multiple agents / subagents within a single task.
capabilities.comparison_note: Anthropic centers definition-native subagents with Custom subagents, Forked subagent, and Worktree isolation; OpenAI takes a workflow-native path through Subagent workflows and Orchestration; Google is lifecycle-native, foregrounding Subagent Lifecycle and States and Inter-Agent Communication.

products.name: Subagents
products.scope_note: Specialized AI assistants that run in their own isolated context window, handle a delegated task, and return a summarized result to the main conversation.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). Subagents are specialized assistants that run in isolated context windows, handle delegated tasks, and return summarized results to the main conversation — a direct match for spawning and coordinating multiple subagen

comparison.summary: Anthropic's Subagents are specialized AI assistants that run in isolated context windows, handle delegated tasks, and return only a summary to the parent conversation — with per-agent tool restrictions, tailored system prompts, and both built-in and configurable variants. Google and OpenAI offer comparable delegation-and-isolation patterns, though with differences in async architecture, configuration mechanisms, and built-in specialization depth.

equivalents.note: Covers both pre-packaged and dynamically defined subagents with custom prompts and tools, closely mirroring Anthropic's built-in vs. configurable subagent model.
Spawns specialized parallel agents and consolidates results, but lacks explicit context isolation and per-agent tool restriction via config frontmatter.

Explore: fast, read-only built-in subagent for searching and analyzing codebases

Custom subagents
products.scope_note: Users can create their own subagents with custom prompts, tool restrictions, permission modes, hooks, and skills.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This is a feature of Claude Code's subagents capability allowing users to define custom subagents with their own prompts, tool restrictions, permission modes, hooks, and skills. It directly fills the subagents-orchest

comparison.summary: Anthropic's Custom Subagents let users define reusable agents with custom prompts, tool restrictions, permission modes, hooks, and skills — stored as markdown files, passed programmatically, or created dynamically at runtime with scoped priority ordering. Google's nearest equivalent supports dynamic custom subagent definition with custom system prompts and tools; OpenAI's equivalent uses TOML files but lacks filesystem-based scoping, hooks, and priority-ordered scope inheritance.

equivalents.note: Supports dynamic custom subagents with custom prompts and tools, but no markdown file definitions, scoped priority ordering, or hooks integration.
TOML-based custom agent definitions with model/instructions config, but no filesystem scoping, scope precedence, hooks, or dynamic runtime creation.

Forked subagent
products.scope_note: A subagent that continues from / inherits the full current conversation context instead of starting fresh.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). A forked subagent is a specific subagent-spawning mechanism that inherits the full current conversation context rather than starting fresh. This is a narrow feature/slice within Claude Code's subagents & orchestration

comparison.summary: Anthropic's Forked Subagent spawns a subagent that inherits the full current conversation context rather than starting with a blank slate. Neither Google nor OpenAI offers a direct equivalent; their subagent mechanisms delegate fresh tasks without forking/inheriting the parent's live conversation context.

equivalents.note: Delegates work to concurrent agents but preserves parent context, not forks it into the subagent.
Spawns specialized parallel agents, but does not fork/inherit the parent conversation context.

products.name: Agent teams
products.scope_note: Coordinate multiple independent Claude Code sessions working together as a team, led by a team lead, with a shared task list and peer-to-peer messaging.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). Agent teams coordinate multiple independent Claude Code sessions working together with a team lead, shared task list, and peer-to-peer messaging — this is squarely multi-agent spawning and coordination, i.e. subagents

comparison.summary: Anthropic's Agent teams lets multiple Claude Code sessions collaborate as a structured team: a lead agent coordinates peers via a shared task list, peer-to-peer messaging, plan approval gates, quality hooks, and flexible terminal display modes. Google's nearest equivalent offers similar multi-agent orchestration with inter-agent messaging but lacks the team-lead/approval-gate model; OpenAI's closest option is more limited, focusing on parallel subagent spawning without structured team roles or a shared task list.

equivalents.note: Covers orchestration and error recovery but lacks explicit team-lead role, shared task list, or plan approval gates.
Parallelizes agents and collects results, but has no team-lead structure, shared task list, or peer messaging.

Dynamic workflows
products.scope_note: A JavaScript script Claude writes that orchestrates subagents at scale and runs in the background, cross-checking their results for work too big to coordinate one turn at a time.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). Dynamic workflows is a Claude-authored JavaScript orchestration script that spawns and coordinates subagents at scale, runs in the background, and cross-checks their results — a direct subagents-orchestration capabili

comparison.summary: Dynamic workflows lets Claude write a JavaScript orchestration script (triggered by keyword or natural language) that runs background multi-agent jobs at scale—up to 1,000 agents per run—with a pre-run approval prompt, live progress view, mid-session pause/resume, and reusable slash-command saving. Google's nearest equivalent covers multi-agent orchestration with error recovery but lacks the scripted workflow-as-code model, approval prompts, and reuse mechanics; OpenAI's Codex subagent workflows parallelize agents but without the structured workflow authoring, phased approval, or progress monitoring.

equivalents.note: Covers orchestration and error recovery, but lacks scripted workflow authoring, phased approval prompts, and slash-command reuse.
Parallelizes agents and consolidates results, but has no workflow-as-script authoring, pre-run approval phases, or progress monitoring UI.

Worktree isolation
products.scope_note: An isolation mode that runs agents in separate git worktrees / checkouts so parallel agents don't overwrite each other's files.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). Worktree isolation is a mechanism enabling multiple parallel agents to operate without overwriting each other's files by giving each its own git worktree/checkout. This is a supporting slice of subagents/orchestration

comparison.summary: Anthropic's Worktree isolation runs each agent session in a separate git worktree (checkout), so parallel agents operate on independent file copies and never conflict. Neither Google nor OpenAI offer an equivalent git-worktree-level file isolation mechanism; their sandbox/project features provide broader environment or folder boundaries but not per-session git checkout isolation.

equivalents.note: Scopes agents to folder/repo boundaries, but does not create separate git worktrees per session.
Defines filesystem boundaries for agents, but has no per-session git worktree isolation.

products.name: isolation: worktree gives a subagent an isolated copy of the repository
Worktrees: separate git checkout per session so parallel sessions never edit the same files
Subagent workflows
products.scope_note: Codex spawns specialized agents in parallel and collects their results in one consolidated response.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This is Codex's capability to spawn specialized agents in parallel and consolidate their results — a direct subagents/orchestration feature. It is a sub-feature of the existing Codex subagents-orchestration product. I

comparison.summary: OpenAI's Codex "Subagent workflows" spawns specialized parallel agents on demand (e.g., one per CSV row) and consolidates their results into a single response, with explicit user triggering required. Both Anthropic and Google offer comparable parallel subagent orchestration, though with differing mechanics around triggering, isolation, and scripting.

equivalents.note: Anthropic's equivalent uses Claude-written JavaScript to orchestrate subagents at scale with cross-checking, vs. OpenAI's declarative CSV-driven spawning.
Google's framework adds built-in error recovery and retries; OpenAI's is more focused on CSV-batch parallelism with explicit triggering.

products.name: Triggering subagent workflows (must be explicitly requested, not spawned automatically)

Orchestration
products.scope_note: Codex handles cross-agent orchestration including spawning, routing instructions, waiting for results, and closing threads.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This offering is Codex's cross-agent orchestration mechanism — spawning subagents, routing instructions, waiting for results, and closing threads — which is squarely the subagents-orchestration capability. It is a sub

comparison.summary: OpenAI's Codex Orchestration manages the full lifecycle of cross-agent coordination: spawning subagents, routing instructions, waiting on results, and closing threads, with sandbox policies and approval requests inherited across inactive threads. Both Anthropic and Google offer comparable multi-agent orchestration, though with differing architectures and levels of built-in automation.

equivalents.note: Coordinates multiple Claude sessions with a team lead, but uses shared task lists rather than thread spawning/routing.
Covers spawning, coordination, and error recovery across agents, closely matching Codex's orchestration scope.

products.name: Approvals and sandbox controls (subagents inherit current sandbox policy and surface approval requests from inactive threads)
Managing subagents
products.scope_note: You can switch between active agent threads and steer, stop, or close running subagents.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This offering covers steering, stopping, closing, and switching between running subagent threads — a control/management slice of the broader subagents-orchestration capability rather than the full spawn-and-coordinate

comparison.summary: OpenAI's "Managing subagents" lets you inspect, switch between, steer, stop, or close running subagent threads from the CLI. Anthropic's nearest match focuses on coordinating multiple sessions rather than direct thread-level control; Google's offering is closer, explicitly modeling subagent lifecycle states and monitoring.

equivalents.note: Coordinates multiple Claude sessions but lacks explicit thread-switching and stop/close controls.
Models subagent states (Running, Idle, Killed) but doesn't emphasize interactive thread-switching from a CLI.

products.name: Agent thread (inspect and switch between agent CLI threads)

comparison.summary: OpenAI's Custom agents lets users define named agents with distinct model configs, reasoning effort levels, and instructions via TOML files, with built-in defaults. equivalents.note: Configured via prompts/tool restrictions, not TOML files; no per-agent model or reasoning effort selection.

Both Anthropic and Google offer comparable multi-agent orchestration frameworks, though with different tooling and infrastructure.

Asynchronous Subagents
products.scope_note: Subagents parallelize complex tasks and preserve the main agent's context by delegating work to dedicated concurrent agents, letting the main agent delegate parallel background tasks without blocking the user's flow.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 0.67). Asynchronous Subagents is a feature for spawning concurrent dedicated subagents to parallelize work and preserve the main agent's context — directly the subagents-orchestration capability. It is a sub-feature of Googl

comparison.summary: Google's Asynchronous Subagents let a main agent fan out parallel background tasks to dedicated concurrent subagents with isolated contexts, avoiding blocking the user's flow. Both Anthropic and OpenAI offer closely comparable multi-agent parallelism features, though with different orchestration models and tooling.

equivalents.note: Coordinates parallel Claude Code sessions as a team, but is lead-directed with a shared task list rather than a pure async fan-out model.
Codex spawns specialized agents in parallel and consolidates results, directly mirroring Google's fan-out async subagent pattern.

products.name: Subagent Lifecycle and States
products.scope_note: Subagents run asynchronously in one of three states: Running, Idle, or Killed.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This describes the lifecycle/state model (Running, Idle, Killed) of subagents running asynchronously — a specific aspect of subagent orchestration rather than the full orchestration capability, hence 'partial'. It is

comparison.summary: Google's Subagent Lifecycle and States defines three explicit runtime states (Running, Idle, Killed) for asynchronously executing subagents, with Auto-Wake allowing idle subagents to resume automatically. Anthropic's nearest match tracks agent coordination without named lifecycle states; OpenAI's "Managing subagents" covers switching and stopping active threads but lacks a formal state model.

equivalents.note: Coordinates multiple async agent sessions but has no explicit Running/Idle/Killed state model.
Lets you switch, steer, or stop threads, but no formal lifecycle states or auto-wake mechanism.

products.name: Inter-Agent Communication
products.scope_note: Agents communicate by sending messages to each other using unique agent IDs, with flexible routing and shared transcripts.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). Inter-agent communication (message passing between agents via unique IDs, flexible routing, shared transcripts) is a mechanism for coordinating multiple agents — a slice of subagents/orchestration rather than the full

comparison.summary: Google's Inter-Agent Communication lets agents address and message each other directly via unique agent IDs, with flexible routing and shared transcripts. Anthropic's nearest equivalent coordinates multiple agents as a team with shared task lists, while OpenAI's orchestration handles cross-agent routing but is orchestrator-driven rather than peer-to-peer.

equivalents.note: Coordinates multiple agents together but uses a lead/peer model, not ID-based direct messaging.
Handles cross-agent routing and spawning, but is orchestrator-directed, not peer-to-peer messaging by agent ID.

products.name: Built-In vs. Custom Subagents
products.scope_note: Antigravity ships pre-packaged specialized subagents and lets agents define custom subagents dynamically with custom system prompts and toolsets.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This is a specific sub-feature of Antigravity's subagents/orchestration capability: it describes the two forms of subagents (pre-packaged built-in specialized subagents vs. dynamically defined custom subagents with cu

comparison.summary: Google Antigravity ships pre-built specialized subagents alongside letting agents dynamically define custom subagents with their own system prompts and toolsets. Both Anthropic and OpenAI offer a comparable custom-subagent definition capability, but neither documents a catalog of pre-packaged built-in subagents the way Antigravity does.

equivalents.note: Covers custom prompts, tool restrictions, and permission modes, but no pre-packaged built-in subagent catalog.
Defines custom agents via TOML files with model configs and instructions; no pre-built subagent library mentioned.

products.name: Multi-Agent Teamwork & Orchestration
products.scope_note: Advanced multi-agent orchestration framework with built-in error recovery, retries, and coordination logic, able to orchestrate agents both synchronously and asynchronously from a central command center.

lifecycle.note: Auto-triage → confirmed (grounding 1.00, classification 1.00). This is a multi-agent orchestration framework focused on coordinating multiple agents (synchronously and asynchronously) with error recovery, retries, and central coordination logic — a direct match for subagents-orch

comparison.summary: Google's Multi-Agent Teamwork & Orchestration provides a centralized framework to coordinate multiple agents with built-in error recovery, retries, and both synchronous and asynchronous execution modes. Anthropic's nearest match is "Agent teams" (multi-session coordination with a team lead), and OpenAI's is "Orchestration" (cross-agent spawning, routing, and result collection via Codex).

equivalents.note: Coordinates multiple Claude sessions under a team lead, but lacks explicit sync/async modes and built-in retry/error recovery logic.
Handles cross-agent spawning, routing, and result collection, but no explicit built-in error recovery or retry framework mentioned.

products.name: orchestrate agents both synchronously and asynchronously
